question: When is indexing a column usually a bad tradeoff according to the notes?

options.text: When reads heavily outnumber writes and latency is query-bound.
options.feedback: Read-heavy selective workloads typically benefit from proper indexing.

options.text: When queries frequently use selective `WHERE` conditions on that column.
options.feedback: That is a strong indexing case because selective reads gain speed.

options.text: When writes dominate and the column is rarely filtered or joined.
options.feedback: Correct: index maintenance adds write overhead without enough query benefit.

options.text: When the column appears in join predicates across large tables.
options.feedback: Join keys are common index targets to reduce expensive table scans.

whyQuestion: Why can an index hurt write-heavy workloads?

whyOptions.text: Because query planners ignore indexed columns for equality comparisons.
whyOptions.feedback: Planners often prefer indexes for selective equality predicates, not ignore them.

whyOptions.text: Because inserts and updates must also maintain index structures on each write.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: every write now updates extra data structures, increasing total cost.

whyOptions.text: Because indexes only accelerate sorting and never help filtering operations.
whyOptions.feedback: Indexes often speed filtering and joins, not just sort operations.

whyOptions.text: Because indexes disable transaction commits until manual vacuum runs.
whyOptions.feedback: Transactions commit normally; index cost is maintenance, not commit disabling.
